meta.title: Realtime feed leaks sockets and listeners
meta.summary: Route mounts and filter changes both create overlapping feed subscriptions, while stale handlers and unclear socket ownership leave old work alive.
signals: Duplicate events grow after route changes and filter tweaks
Some duplicate logs still mention old filter values
Connection and listener counts keep climbing in dev tools

context.symptom: A trading feed works at first, but after switching dashboards and tweaking filters a few times, each incoming event appears multiple times and some duplicates use old filter logic.
context.userImpact: The page becomes noisy and slower over time, especially for users who navigate between dashboards.
context.environment: React route mounts a feed component that subscribes to a shared socket helper, while an effect reruns whenever the filters object identity changes.

evidence.title: Heap growth
evidence.body: Heap grows by roughly 8MB after each open-close cycle and never returns to baseline.

evidence.title: Duplicate handler clues
evidence.body: After switching from 'all' to 'critical', some duplicate events still log the old 'all' filter value from a stale handler closure.

evidence.title: Effect excerpt
Connection count
evidence.body: Dev tools sometimes show one long-lived socket, but listener count and server subscriptions still climb after each route revisit.

stages.title: Stage 1: likely root cause
stages.prompt: What explains the leak most directly?
stages.helperText: Choose the explanation that fits duplicate events, stale filter closures, and confused connection ownership.

options.label: The feed volume is just high enough that repeated messages look like a leak.
options.feedback: High volume can make the page noisy, but it does not explain retained handlers using old filter values.

options.label: StrictMode double-invocation is the issue, so production cleanup does not matter.
options.feedback: StrictMode can reveal lifecycle issues, but the real problem is missing cleanup around external subscriptions.

options.label: Socket and listener ownership is unclear: the effect reruns on filter changes, adds new handlers and subscriptions, and cleanup never fully tears down the old work.
options.feedback: Correct. This is broader than 'forgot cleanup once'. The design allows overlapping listeners, stale closures, and sometimes overlapping subscriptions.

options.label: The server is probably sending duplicates, so the client lifecycle is mostly fine.
options.feedback: Server duplicates are possible in real life, but the stale filter closures and rising listener count point back to client-side subscription ownership too.

stages.title: Stage 2: debug priority order
stages.prompt: Rank the three most effective debug steps first.
stages.helperText: Start by proving what stays alive across both route churn and filter churn.

candidates.label: Repeat route mount-unmount cycles and filter changes while counting active listeners or subscriptions after each pass.
Inspect connection ownership, effect dependencies, and whether cleanup unsubscribes and removes the exact handler it created.
Use logs to confirm old handlers still receive events with previous filter values after the UI has moved on.
Compress socket payloads before checking lifecycle behavior.
Raise browser memory thresholds so the app stops slowing down.

stages.title: Stage 3: best fix set
stages.prompt: Which changes belong in the first reliable fix?
stages.helperText: Choose the options that make connection ownership explicit and stop handler accumulation.

options.label: Suppress duplicate message warnings in the console to keep the UI calm.
options.feedback: That hides the signal instead of fixing the retained subscriptions.

options.label: Return cleanup that unsubscribes and removes the exact handler created by the effect before filters or route state change.
options.feedback: Correct. External subscriptions must be torn down with the same identity they were created with.

options.label: Separate long-lived socket ownership from per-view listeners so one layer owns the connection and views only subscribe or unsubscribe.
options.feedback: Correct. Shared connections are safer when one owner manages lifecycle and children do not reopen the world.

options.label: Create the socket in render so React always keeps it in sync with props.
options.feedback: Opening sockets during render makes lifecycle bugs worse, not better.

options.label: Narrow or stabilize dependencies so harmless filter object churn does not recreate listeners or subscriptions.
options.feedback: Correct. Broad dependency churn often turns an ordinary feed into a subscription storm.

stages.title: Stage 4: regression guard
stages.prompt: What is the best follow-up guardrail?
stages.helperText: Pick the safeguard that catches leaks before users notice them.

options.label: Add a route-plus-filter churn regression test and listener or subscription-count instrumentation so stale handlers become obvious immediately.
options.feedback: Correct. It protects the exact messy path that caused the bug, not just memory in the abstract.

options.label: Have someone manually refresh the page monthly to watch for memory growth.
options.feedback: Manual checks are too weak for a lifecycle leak that can recur with small code changes.

options.label: Track only feed latency because slower messages caused the duplicates.
options.feedback: Latency is useful, but it does not catch duplicate listeners or retained sockets.

options.label: Force a full page reload after every navigation away from the feed.
options.feedback: That hides the leak by resetting the app instead of fixing subscription ownership.

scoreBands.label: Leak identified too vaguely
scoreBands.summary: You saw slowness, but not the retained subscription lifecycle behind it.

scoreBands.label: Correct diagnosis, softer guardrail
scoreBands.summary: You found the cleanup issue, but the debug order or long-term guard missed one lever.

scoreBands.label: Lifecycle ownership mindset
scoreBands.summary: You named the ownership problem, removed stale subscriptions, and protected both route churn and filter churn.

idealRunbook: Reproduce route churn and filter churn together.
Measure active listeners, subscriptions, and stale handler behavior.
Clarify who owns the socket versus who only subscribes.
Add instrumentation that makes duplicate listeners impossible to miss.

teachingBlocks.title: Common interview trap
teachingBlocks.text: Many candidates say 'memory leak' and stop. The stronger answer names what stayed alive, who owned it, and why the lifecycle let it survive.

teachingBlocks.title: Stronger explanation
items: Cleanup removes the same handler identity that subscribed.
Dependencies reflect connection identity, not every render-shaped object.

debrief.optionalReflectionPrompt: If one global socket stayed open for the whole app, where would you draw the boundary between connection ownership and per-screen subscriptions?
